coredata.dc:title: First-principles study on the modulation mechanism of solvent effect on the fluorescence emission of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Xiaojie
Guan, Li
Shi, Weicai
Zhang, Jianen
Chen, Mingjun
Luo, Tao
Liu, Chunzhi
Lan, Youshi
Chen, Zhijuan
Chen, Xiaobo
Li, Xu
coredata.dc:description: Solvent effect is one of the effective pathways to regulate the fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots (CDs). In this work, the solvent effect on fluorescence emission of CDs with functional groups has been investigated using density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) methods. Solvation model based on density (SMD) and microsolvent-SMD models have been employed to simulate the solvent effects. The electron donating or withdrawing ability of the surface functional groups of CDs, the polarity of the solvent, and the interaction between solvent molecules and CDs were analyzed. The results indicate that microsolvent-SMD model comprehensively considers the solvent polarity and the interaction between solvent molecules and functional groups, which is more advantageous to revealing the physical nature of solvent molecules participating in fluorescence emission, resulting in the calculated fluorescence spectra closer to experimental results. The hydrogen bonds can modulate the fluorescence spectra of CDs more significantly than the solvent polarity, when the CDs are primarily characterized by n-π* transitions, hydrogen bonding induces a blue shift in the fluorescence spectrum, whereas when the CDs mainly undergo π-π* transitions, hydrogen bonding causes a red shift in the spectrum. This work provides theoretical guidance to exploit the solvent effect to achieve effective modulation of the fluorescence properties of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solvent effect
Carbon dots
First-principles method
Fluorescence spectra
Solvatochromism phenomena

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dots models and calculation methods
Fluorescence spectra of anthracene modified by electron-donating functional groups
Fluorescence spectra of anthracene modified by electron-withdrawing functional groups.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62075055, No. 62175075, No. 61974009), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. 2020201030), the Central Project Guide Local Science and Technology for Development of Hebei Province (226Z1103G, 216Z1104G), the Interdisciplinary research project of Hebei University (No. DXK202012), the Hebei Province Optoelectronic Information Materials Laboratory Performance Subsidy Fund Project (No. 22567634H), and the College students innovation and entrepreneurship training program of Hebei University (DC2024141). We also appreciate High Performance Supercomputer Center of Hebei University.

This work was partly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62075055 , No. 62175075 , No. 61974009 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. 2020201030 ), the Central Project Guide Local Science and Technology for Development of Hebei Province ( 226Z1103G , 216Z1104G ), and the Interdisciplinary research project of Hebei University (No. DXK202012 ). We also appreciate High Performance Supercomputer Center of Hebei University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) CDs structures used in SMD model. (b) Modified anthracene with electron-donating functional group used in microsolvation-SMD model. (c) Modified anthracene electron-withdrawing functional group used in microsolvation-SMD model. Here, the solvent molecules are represented by balls. White ball = H atom, gray ball = C atom, blue ball = N atom, red ball = O atom, green ball = Cl atom.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs modified with electron-donating functional groups in three solvents CYH, DCM and MA using the SMD model and microsolvation-SMD model. (a) Dimethylamino-modified anthracene; (b) Amino-modified anthracene; (c) Hydroxyl-modified anthracene. Here, the dotted and solid lines represent the spectra calculated by using the SMD model and microsolvation-SMD model, respectively.

Calculated HOMO-LUMO gaps of anthracene modified with electron-donating functional groups based on the SMD model and microsolvation-SMD model. Here, “Δ” represents the energy level calculated by the SMD model, and “─” represents the energy level by the microsolvation-SMD model.

(a) The calculated hole-electron distribution plot (iso = 0.002) and (b) Chole-Cele plot (iso = 0.0015) of electron-donating functional group modified anthracene based on the microsolvation-SMD model. The green and blue regions represent electron and hole, respectively.

Visualized interaction for electron-donating functional group modified anthracene based on the microsolvation-SMD model. The green regions represent van der Waals, blue regions represent hydrogen bonds and yellow lines represent the interaction paths.

Atomic heat maps and the corresponding atomic structures of electron-donating functional group modified anthracene based on the microsolvation-SMD model. (a) and (b) Dimethylamino-modified anthracene; (c) and (d) Amino-modified anthracene; (e) and (f) Hydroxyl-modified anthracene.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs modified with electron-withdrawing functional groups in three solvents CYH, DCM and MA using the SMD model and microsolvation-SMD model. (a) Aldehyde-modified anthracene; (b) Ketone-modified anthracene; (c) Carboxyl-modified anthracene. Here, the dotted and solid lines represent the spectra calculated by using the SMD model and microsolvation-SMD model, respectively.

Calculated HOMO-LUMO gaps of anthracene modified with electron-withdrawing functional groups based on the SMD model and microsolvation-SMD model. Here, “Δ” represents the energy level calculated by the SMD model, and “─” represents the energy level calculated by the microsolvation-SMD model.

Visualized interaction for electron-withdrawing functional group modified anthracene based on the microsolvation-SMD model. The green regions represent van der Waals, blue regions represent hydrogen bonds and yellow lines represent the interaction paths.

Atomic heat maps and the corresponding atomic structures of electron-withdrawing functional group modified anthracene based on the microsolvation-SMD model. (a) and (b) Aldehyde-modified anthracene; (c) and (d) Ketone-modified anthracene; (e) and (f) Carboxyl-modified anthracene.

Comparison of experimental and calculated fluorescence spectra of ketone-modified anthracene structure. In order to focus on the red shift of the fluorescence emission in different solvents, the calculated emission peak was corrected for 115 nm blue shift based on the measured data in experiments.

Calculated charge transfer distance (D, Å), separation degree (t, Å), overlap degree (Sr ), charge transfer number (CT) of electron-donating functional group modified anthracene based on the SMD and microsolvation-SMD model. Here, (*) (* = CYH, DCM, MA) represents the results obtained by SMD model, (*, M) represents the results obtained by microsolvation-SMD model.

head.ce:title: First-principles study on the modulation mechanism of solvent effect on the fluorescence emission of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province, College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province
College of Physics Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of High-precision Computation and Application of Quantum Field Theory of Hebei Province, College of Physics Science and Technology, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiochemistry, China Institute of Atomic Energy, Beijing 102413, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Radiochemistry, China Institute of Atomic Energy, Beijing 102413, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Energy, Matter, and Systems, School of Science and Engineering, University of Missouri-Kansas City, KS, MO 64110, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Energy, Matter, and Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Energy, Matter, and Systems, School of Science and Engineering, University of Missouri-Kansas City, KS, MO 64110, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The modulation mechanism of CDs fluorescence emission by solvent effects was investigated using a first-principles method. The results indicate that solvent polarity and the interaction between solvents and CDs synergistically affect the fluorescence properties of CDs. Specifically, hydrogen bonding has a more significant influence on modulating the fluorescence spectra of CDs compared to solvent polarity.

Solvent effect is one of the effective pathways to regulate the fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots (CDs). In this work, the solvent effect on fluorescence emission of CDs with functional groups has been investigated using density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) methods. Solvation model based on density (SMD) and microsolvent-SMD models have been employed to simulate the solvent effects. The electron donating or withdrawing ability of the surface functional groups of CDs, the polarity of the solvent, and the interaction between solvent molecules and CDs were analyzed. The results indicate that microsolvent-SMD model comprehensively considers the solvent polarity and the interaction between solvent molecules and functional groups, which is more advantageous to revealing the physical nature of solvent molecules participating in fluorescence emission, resulting in the calculated fluorescence spectra closer to experimental results. The hydrogen bonds can modulate the fluorescence spectra of CDs more significantly than the solvent polarity, when the CDs are primarily characterized by n-π* transitions, hydrogen bonding induces a blue shift in the fluorescence spectrum, whereas when the CDs mainly undergo π-π* transitions, hydrogen bonding causes a red shift in the spectrum. This work provides theoretical guidance to exploit the solvent effect to achieve effective modulation of the fluorescence properties of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials composed of sp2 hybrid carbon cores and surface functional groups [1,2]. Due to unique photoelectric properties, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and good water solubility [3–6], CDs are widely used in many fields such as environmental monitoring, biological imaging and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [7–13]. The fluorescence intensity and spectral range of CDs are adjustable, and thus the multicolor emission can be easily achieved [14,15]. The factors affecting the fluorescence properties of CDs mainly include environmental effect, quantum confinement effect, crosslinking polymerization, doping atoms, and molecular states [16–20]. The environmental effect mainly refers to solvent effects, temperature and pH value of the solution [21–23]. It is widely known that CDs are commonly prepared and applied in solution, and the solvent effect is one of the effective pathways to modulate the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. Therefore, it is crucial to study the solvent effects on the fluorescence properties of CDs and to achieve controllable modulation of CDs [24,25].

Wang et al. synthesized CDs using p-phenylenediamine, and found that a positive solvatochromism effect appeared when the samples were dispersed in various solvents, causing a modulation of fluorescence emission from green to red as solvent polarity increased [26]. Isobe et al. prepared phloroglucinol-derived CD (Ph-CD), and the phenomenon of negative solvatochromism was observed when the Ph-CD powder was dispersed in solvents with varying polarities [27]. It is believed that both positive and negative solvatochromism phenomena are attributed to the effect of the solvent polarity [26,27]. However, recent experiments have shown that the fluorescence emission of CDs is mainly modulated by the hydrogen bonds formed between the solvent and the CDs, rather than the solvent polarity. Ren et al. dispersed CD into different solvents and found that the fluorescence spectra of CD were similar, suggesting that the hydrogen bonding between the alcohol solvents and the CD was the primary cause of spectral modulation [28]. Fan et al. also suggested that the shift in the fluorescence spectrum was mainly caused by hydrogen bonding, because they found that as the solvent polarity increased, the surface of CDs received negative charges from hydrogen bonding groups, increasing the electron cloud density and leading to a red shift of the CDs [29].

The above experiments have confirmed the existence of the solvatochromism phenomenon in the synthesis of CDs, but the explanations about the solvent effects are inconsistent. Therefore, it is necessary to further analyze the physical nature of the solvent effect. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) are commonly used to explore this issue. Niu et al. calculated the absorption and emission mechanism of N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) by TD-DFT method, and investigated the influence of toluene and tetrahydrofuran solvents on GQD based on the implicit conductor like solvation model (COSMO). Their results showed that the larger the polarity of the solvent, the greater the red shift of the absorption and emission spectra, and the higher the fluorescence intensity [30]. Yang et al. used the DFT method to simulate 10 solvents such as toluene, acetone, and methanol through the implicit polarization continuum model (PCM), and it was found that the increase of the solvent polarity reduced the band gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the CD structure, accelerated the π-π* transition from S1 to S0 state, and led to fluorescence emission red shift [31]. Zheng et al. performed DFT calculations by using an implicit PCM solvent model and found that with the increase of the solvent polarity, the solvent effect became more obvious [32].

All the above theoretical works were performed based on the implicit solvent models, and the results can help understand the influence of the solvent polarity on the fluorescence emission of CDs. However, the implicit model cannot explain the significant modulation effect of solvent on the fluorescence spectrum observed in many experiments, and cannot clearly describe the interaction between CDs and solvent molecules. To date, the microscopic interaction between dispersed solvents and CDs remains unclear [24,33]. In order to comprehensively explore the influence of solvent effect on the fluorescence properties of CDs, anthracene structures modified with electron-donating and electron-withdrawing functional groups are constructed in this work. Using DFT and TD-DFT methods, the fluorescence spectra and electronic properties of CDs in various solvents are calculated. Here, the solvation model based on the density (SMD) model and the microsolvent-SMD model are employed for describing the solvent effects [34]. This work reveals the modulation mechanism of the solvent effect on the fluorescence properties of CDs modified with various surface functional groups and provides theoretical guidance for the controlled synthesis of CDs in solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots models and calculation methods
ce:section.ce:para: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are commonly used structural models to study the luminescence mechanism of CDs. Many previous theoretical works have successfully used structures such as anthracene, pyrene, coronene, circumcoronene and other PAHs structures to analyze the effects of N-doping and graphene oxide on the absorption spectra and fluorescence properties [30,31,35,36]. Any theoretical model has certain limitations. There are four mechanisms established to study the fluorescence properties of CDs, including quantum confinement effect, surface functional groups, molecular state and cross-linking enhanced emission effect. The PAHs models are more suitable for describing the CDs whose fluorescence properties are mainly modulated by surface functional groups. Although the PAHs structures may not be direct components of CDs, their molecular structures and electronic properties provide a theoretical basis for understanding the fluorescence properties of CDs.

Anthracene is a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon containing three benzene rings, and can be seen as a luminescent center of CDs. Cao et al. used anthracene as a CD structure to study the effect of the substitution position of functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups on the fluorescence emission wavelength [37]. Matsika et al. studied the effects of mono-substitution, di-substitution, and tetra-substitution on the absorption and fluorescence properties of anthracene, indicating that dopant sites can be used to effectively adjust the photophysical properties of fluorescent materials [38]. Fu et al. used the anthracene structure as the fluorescence center to simulate the fluorescence properties of CDs, indicating that the excitation dependence of CDs is likely because CDs contain PAHs of different sizes and functionalized aromatic hydrocarbons [39]. These studies illustrate that the anthracene structure has the advantages of easy modification and adjustable fluorescence, which can provide useful insights into the electronic structure of CDs or at least its structural components.

The present work uses anthracene as the basic CD structure. In the anthracene structure, positions 1 and 4 are relatively reactive in chemical reactions [37], so the CDs modified by functional groups were constructed by replacing the H atoms at positions 1 and 4. Generally, N-containing and O-containing functional groups are most common in the synthesis of CDs, therefore, six surface functional groups, dimethylamino (−N(CH3)2), amino (–NH2), hydroxyl (–OH), aldehyde (–CHO), ketone(–COCH3) and carboxyl (–COOH), were selected to modify anthracene [40–42]. According to the electronic properties, the six functional groups are categorized into electron-donating functional groups (−N(CH3)2, –NH2, –OH) and electron-withdrawing functional groups (–CHO, –COCH3, –COOH). Table S1 demonstrates the electron-donating and electron-withdrawing abilities of these functional groups. The order of electron-donating ability from strong to weak is −N(CH3)2 > –NH2 > –OH, and the order of electron-withdrawing ability from strong to weak is –CHO > –COCH3 > –COOH. In this work, three common solvents, cyclohexane (CYH), dichloromethane (DCM), and methanol (MA), were selected. CYH is a non-polar solvent, DCM is a medium-polar solvent (3.4), and MA is a high-polar solvent (6.4).

The interaction between surface functional groups of CDs and solvents has a critical impact on the fluorescence color of CDs.[43–45] Because the implicit SMD model does not consider the interaction between solvent molecules and CDs, a microsolvent-SMD model taking into account solvent molecules was built in this work in order to better understand the solvent effect. Fig. 1 (a) shows six CD structures constructed for the calculation of the SMD model. Fig. 1(b) and (c) show the CD structures with the solvent molecules constructed for the calculation of the microsolvent-SMD model. Here, the microsolvent-SMD model can overcome the shortcomings of the implicit model and beneficially analyze the interaction between solvent molecules and CD. This microsolvent method has been successfully applied to the study of Cu4(L)4‧2EtOH (L=5-(4-pyridyl) tetrazole) and betaine in the field of metal-organic framework and dyes [46–48]. In order to accurately construct the system containing CDs and solvent molecules, the electrostatic potential analysis and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of CDs and solvent molecules were performed (see Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). The results show that the binding positions of solvent molecules and CDs obtained by MD simulations are consistent with the ones obtained by electrostatic potential analysis, suggesting that the constructed CD-solvent models are reliable.

All theoretical calculation computations were performed by the Gaussian 16 package [49], the excited state electronic properties of all structures were analyzed using the Multiwfn 3.8 package [50], and all isosurface plots were drawn and render using the VMD visualization program [51]. All calculations were performed with DFT and TD-DFT methods using the B3LYP exchange-correlation functional with the 6-311G(d) basis set [52–54]. D3 dispersion corrections with Becke-Johnson-damping were used to describe the dispersion force [55]. Firstly, optimization of the ground state structure, energy calculation, and frequency analysis of all CDs models were carried out using the DFT method. Then, the first excited state structures were optimized by using the TD-DFT method to obtain the fluorescence spectra. In order to verify the accuracy of the calculation method and make a preliminary comparison of the SMD model and the microsolvent-SMD model, 2-(dimethylamino) phenazine with a similar structure to anthracene was selected for testing. The results show that the calculated spectra using the microsolvent-SMD model are more consistent with the experimentally-measured absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs in solvents such as CYH, DCM, and EtOH [32], and the details of the comparison are shown in Fig. S1. The testing manifests that the calculation methods used in this work are reasonable. Both the SMD model and the microsolvent-SMD model are used in all the following calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra of anthracene modified by electron-donating functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we calculated the fluorescence spectra of anthracene structures modified by electron-donating functional groups (−N(CH3)2, –NH2, –OH) in three solvents. The results are shown in Fig. 2 . The dotted lines in Fig. 2 show the fluorescence spectra calculated using the SMD model. It can be seen that, for the same CD structure, the fluorescence spectra of CDs gradually red shift with the increase of the solvent polarity. The excited-state dipole moments of the three CDs in different solvents are given in Table S2, and the results indicate that with the increase of the solvent polarity, the direction of the dipole moment does not change, but the magnitude increases. Among the three solvents, the MA solvent with the highest polarity exhibits the largest dipole moment.

Here, in order to emphasize the relative shift of spectra in different solvents, the spectra in the non-polar CYH solvent are set as a reference. From the CYH solvent to the MA solvent, the fluorescence spectra of dimethylamino-modified, amino-modified, and hydroxyl-modified anthracene structures show redshifts of 58, 52, and 40 nm, respectively. The results show that for the same change in the solvent polarity, the stronger the electron-donating ability of the surface functional groups, the more pronounced the red shift of the fluorescence emission of CDs. Thus, the dimethylamino-modified CD structure exhibits the greatest fluorescence emission red shift.

The solid lines in Fig. 2 display the calculated fluorescence spectra of CDs based on the microsolvent-SMD model. Due to taking into account the interaction between CDs and solvent molecules, so these calculation results are different from those of the implicit SMD model. Here, the spectra in the non-polar CYH solvent are set as a reference. Fig. 2(a) shows the fluorescence spectra of the dimethylamino-modified anthracene. One can see that in the MA solvent, the spectrum calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model exhibits a blue shift of 52 nm, which is significantly different from the red-shifted fluorescence spectrum calculated by the SMD model. Fig. 2(b) and (c) show the fluorescence spectra of amino- and hydroxyl-modified anthracene structures, respectively. The results show that the fluorescence spectra of the two CDs calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model exhibit a more obvious red shift than those calculated by the SMD model. It is thus clear that due to considering the interaction between CDs and solvent molecules, the microsolvent-SMD model is more helpful in revealing the influence of the solvent on the fluorescence emission of CDs.

Analyzing the configuration interaction contribution indicates that the transition contributions from HOMO to LUMO of these structures are about 99% (see Table S3), suggesting that the fluorescence emission from the first excited state S1 to the ground state S0 is mainly dominated by the transition from HOMO to LUMO. Fig. 3 shows the HOMO-LUMO gaps of all CD structures calculated by the SMD model and the microsolvent-SMD model. It can be seen that the HOMO and LUMO energy levels calculated by the SMD model decrease with the increase of the solvent polarity, and the LUMO energy level is more sensitive to the change of the solvent polarity. Therefore, the high-polar MA solvent causes the LUMO energy level to decrease the most, and finally, the HOMO-LUMO gaps of three CDs are the smallest in MA solvent. The “─” in Fig. 3 represents the energy level of CDs calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model. It is found that the change in the HOMO-LUMO gap is significantly greater than the results of the SMD model. The interaction of functional groups with solvent molecules is considered, so the polarity of the solvent is no longer the only factor affecting the energy level. The microsolvent-SMD model can reflect the charge exchange between CDs and solvent molecules, providing a more accurate prediction of electron energy level of CDs.

In order to evaluate the contribution of molecular orbitals to electronic transitions, the hole-electron distribution of electron-donating functional group modified anthracene was further analyzed. From the hole-electron distribution plot and Chole-Cele plot calculated by the SMD model (Fig. S4), one can see that the distribution ranges of holes and electrons are clearly distinguished, indicating that the fluorescence emission of these three CDs belong to charge transfer excitation.

The results obtained by the microsolvent-SMD model also show that the fluorescence emission of CDs is dominantly attributed to the transition from HOMO to LUMO (see Table S4). The specific physical quantities of the hole-electron distribution calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model are given in Table 1. It can be found that dimethylamino and amino have strong electron-donating ability, and thus the CDs modified with the two functional groups have greater values of CT. This result means that the stronger the electron-donating ability of the surface functional group of CDs, the greater the degree of charge transfer. Besides, the microsolvent-SMD model makes it clear that the solvent molecule has a significant influence on the hole-electron distribution of CDs. For the same CD in the three solvents, almost no difference was observed in the hole-electron separation degree calculated by the SMD model. In contrast, the result calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model shows significant differences. For example, the t value of the dimethylamino-modified anthracene structure in the CYH solvent is 0.730 Å, while it is only 0.082 Å in the MA solvent.

Fig. 4 shows the results of the hole-electron distribution plot and Chole-Cele plot[56] of CDs calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model. It can be seen from Fig. 4(a), that the solvent polarity and the interaction between CDs and solvent molecules synergistically affect the hole-electron distribution of CDs. For the dimethylamino-modified anthracene structure in high-polar MA solvent, the charge is delocalized to the solvent molecules. This results in a remarkable change in the electron energy levels. The hydroxyl-modified anthracene structure has more holes distributed on the benzene ring far away from the functional groups in CYH and DCM solvents, while the hole distribution on this benzene ring is significantly reduced in the MA solvent. This indicates that the high-polar MA solvent leads to a more apparent separation of holes and electrons in the CDs.

In Fig. 4(b), when the solvent molecules are located on the surface and beneath the CDs, the distribution range of electrons and holes begins to expand in the direction perpendicular to the plane of the CDs; when the solvent molecules are at the edge of the CDs, the distribution range of electrons and holes only expands on the carbon domain plane. These results show that charge exchange occurs between solvent molecules and functional groups. The position of solvent molecules significantly affects the degree of charge transfer and the distribution of holes and electrons. This manifests that solvent molecules participate in the fluorescence emission process. Compared with the SMD model, the microsolvent-SMD model can better reveal the influence of solvent molecules on the distribution of hole and electron, and truly reflect the physical nature of solvent participating in fluorescence emission of CDs.

In order to further clarify the interaction between CDs and solvent molecules, the Independent Gradient Model Based on Hirshfeld Partition (IGMH)[57] method was used to perform visual analysis of the interaction. The results are shown in Fig. 5 . Here, the CDs and the solvents are divided into two independent entities, and the interaction between the two parts is visualized. The specific coloring method of sign(λ2)ρ in different value ranges is shown in Fig. S5.

Fig. 5 illustrates the interaction analysis of the anthracene structures modified by electron-donating functional groups (−N(CH3)2, –NH2, –OH) in three solvents. In the non-polar CYH and medium-polar DCM solvents, only van der Waals forces exist between solvent molecules and CDs; in high-polar MA solvent, in addition to van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds are observed. In Fig. 5(a), it is clearly observed that N…H-O hydrogen bonds are formed between the MA molecules and the N atoms in the dimethylamino functional groups. In Fig. 5(b)-(c), N-O…H and O-H…O hydrogen bonds are formed between the MA molecules and the H atoms in amino and hydroxyl groups, respectively.

In order to further explore the impact of solvent molecules on the charge distribution, the atomic heat maps were calculated, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . It can be found that the interaction between solvent molecules and CDs mainly affects the N and O atoms of the functional groups and the C atoms in the benzene ring. Fig. 6(a) illustrates that in the three solvents, the most dominant contributions to the electronic transition are from the N15 and N16, and C7 and C10 in the dimethylamino-modified anthracene structure. In the MA solvent, N…H-O hydrogen bonds are formed between the MA molecules and the dimethylamino groups. The atomic heat maps indicates that from the CYH solvent to the MA solvent, the color corresponding to N15 and N16 changes from orange to yellow, indicating a decrease in the holes in the functional groups and a decrease in the degree of charge transfer. This is attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between N atoms, which possess lone pair electrons, and the MA solvent. Such hydrogen bonding diminishes the contribution of N in the functional groups to the electronic transition, altering the charge distribution and the overall charge transfer within the CDs. Correspondingly, the HOMO energy level decreases, and the energy required for the n-π* transition of S0-S1 increases. This widens the HOMO-LUMO gap, ultimately resulting in a blue shift of the fluorescence emission from the dimethylamino-modified anthracene structure in MA solvent (shown in Fig. 2).

The atomic heat maps of the amino- and hydroxyl-modified anthracene structures are illustrated in Fig. 6(c) and (e). With the increase of the solvent polarity, the orange color of the N atom on the amino group gradually becomes darker, the O atom on the hydroxyl group changes from blue to green, and the hole content on the C7 and C10 decreases. The charge exchange exists between CDs and the solvent when N-H…O or O-H…O hydrogen bonds are formed. The large spectral shift and significant changes in atomic contribution caused by the hydrogen bonds. Due to the fact that the microsolvent-SMD model only consider the interaction between solvent molecules and CDs and does not take into account the interaction between solvent molecules, the contribution of hydrogen bonds will be possibly overestimated [58,59]. To construct the more accurate structural models and avoid the overestimation of hydrogen bonds, the relationship between the spectral shift and the number of hydrogen bonds between the solvent molecule and the CDs has been investigated. The models and calculation results are shown in Table S5, and one can see that when the microsolvent-SMD model is used to calculate the solvent effect, the number of solvent molecules should be carefully selected to reduce the possible overestimation of hydrogen bonds, and the electrostatic potential analysis and MD simulation calculation need to be done first.

According to the above discussions about the fluorescence spectra and electronic properties, the results calculated via the SMD model show that as the solvent polarity increases, the excited-state dipole moment and charge transfer degree of the CDs increase, the band gaps of the CDs decrease, and the fluorescence spectra exhibit a red shift. In contrast, the microsolvent-SMD model takes into account the interaction between CDs and solvent molecules. Upon visualization of the interaction, it is evident that in non-polar or medium-polar solvents, only weak van der Waals forces are formed between the solvent molecules and the CDs. However, in the high-polar MA solvent, hydrogen bonds are formed, which significantly impact the fluorescence emission by altering the charge transfer of CDs. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that hydrogen bonds play a prominent role in modulating fluorescence spectra than the solvent polarity. The results declare that the interaction between CDs and solvents is of great significance in explaining the modulation mechanism of solvent effect on fluorescence emission.

Recently, Zhang et al. prepared the red-emitting CDs modified with amino functional groups [31]. They dispersed the samples in different solvents and found that the fluorescence emission red shift of CDs is about 40 nm from DCM solvent to MA solvent. The present work shows that the calculated fluorescence emissions of the amino-modified anthracene structure based on the SMD model and the microsolvent-SMD model are red shifted by 13 and 41 nm, respectively. Thus, the result calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model is more in line with the experimental measurement. Therefore, the microsolvent-SMD model gives a better description about the influence of the solvent effect on the fluorescence properties of CDs by comprehensively considering the solvent polarity and the interaction between the solvent molecules and the electron-donating functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra of anthracene modified by electron-withdrawing functional groups.
ce:section.ce:para: In order to fully understand the solvent effect on various CDs, the fluorescence spectra and electronic properties of the anthracene structures modified with electron-withdrawing functional groups (–CHO, –COCH3, –COOH) were also studied. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence spectra of three CD structures in CYH, DCM, and MA solvents were calculated based on the SMD model and the microsolvent-SMD model.

Firstly, the fluorescence spectra calculated by the SMD model were analyzed, as shown by the dotted lines in Fig. 7. It can be found that for the same CD, the fluorescence spectrum gradually red shifts as the polarity of the solvent increases. The spectra in the non-polar CYH solvent are set as a reference. From CYH to DCM or MA solvent, the fluorescence spectrum of the carboxyl-modified anthracene structure has the smallest red shift. This is closely related to the weak electron-withdrawing ability of the carboxyl. The excited state dipole moment of CDs increases as the solvent polarity increases (as shown in Table S6). This makes the excited state more stable, resulting in a red shift of the fluorescence spectrum. However, in contrast to the fluorescence properties of anthracene modified by electron-donating functional groups (shown as Fig. 2), the three anthracene structures modified by electron-withdrawing functional groups have intramolecular hydrogen bonds, such as O…H-C hydrogen bond (2.1 Å) formed between O atom of carboxyl and H atom of anthracene. These intramolecular hydrogen bonds enhance the stability of the CD structure and reduce the influence of the solvent polarity on the fluorescence emission to a certain extent.

The calculated fluorescence spectra based on the microsolvent-SMD model are shown by the solid lines in Fig. 7. One can see that all three CDs exhibit a positive solvatochromism phenomenon in all three solvents. From the non-polar CYH solvent to the high-polar MA solvent, the fluorescence spectra of aldehyde-modified, ketone-modified, and carboxyl-modified anthracene structures show red shifts of 109, 99, and 47 nm, respectively. For example, the fluorescence emission of the ketone-modified anthracene structure is shifted from 615 to 714 nm, and the corresponding fluorescence color is from orange to red, which demonstrates an obvious solvent modulation effect. However, it is interesting that the degree of spectral red shift does not fully depend on the solvent polarity. Compared to the fluorescence spectra of the three CDs in CYH solvent, those in the DCM solvent show remarkable red shifts, which is due to the formation of weak hydrogen bonds or halogen bonds between the DCM solvent molecules and the CDs.

Comparing the calculation results of the SMD model and the microsolvent-SMD model, it is found that both the two models reveal that as the solvent polarity increases, the fluorescence spectra of CD gradually red shift. However, the degree of spectral red shift calculated by SMD model is too little to fully explain the mechanism of the positive solvatochromism phenomenon in the CD experiment [60,61], while the microsolvent-SMD model can provide an adequate description of the solvent effect.

The fluorescence emission of the anthracene structure modified with electron-withdrawing functional groups is also mainly contributed by the transition from HOMO to LUMO (as shown in Table S7), and the HOMO-LUMO gaps of these structures were calculated. The results are shown in Fig. 8 . Based on the SMD solvent model, the calculated bandgaps of CDs decrease with the increase of the solvent polarity. Also, the stronger the electron-withdrawing ability of the functional group, the smaller the bandgap of CDs, as shown by the “Δ” part in Fig. 8. From the CYH to MA solvent, the band gaps of aldehyde-modified, ketone-modified, and carboxyl-modified anthracene structures decrease by 0.250, 0.192, and 0.041 eV, respectively. The electron-withdrawing ability of the carboxyl group is the weakest, so the red shift of the fluorescence emission is minimal. In other words, the results based on the SMD solvent model show that as the polarity of the solvent increases, the stronger the electron-withdrawing ability of the CDs functional group, the greater the red shift of the CD's fluorescence emission.

The HOMO and LUMO energy levels calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model are also given in Fig. 8. Due to the strong electron-withdrawing ability of aldehyde and ketone groups, as the solvent polarity increases, the HOMO energy level of CDs increases, while the LUMO energy level decreases, and finally the bandgap decreases. However, for the carboxyl-modified anthracene structure, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels change slightly, which is associated with the intramolecular hydrogen bonds weakening the interaction between CDs and solvents to a certain extent. From the frontier molecular orbitals calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model (see Fig. S6), one can see that all three CDs’ fluorescence emissions originate from the π-π* transition. In the high-polar MA solvent, the electrons are delocalized to the MA molecules, causing an obvious change in HOMO and LUMO energy levels. From the hole-electron distribution calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model (see Table S8), it can be found that as the solvent polarity and the electron-withdrawing ability of the functional group increase, the interaction between CDs and solvent molecules increases, the charge transfer degree of CDs increases, and finally the fluorescence emission red shift is aggravated.

By using IGMH method, the interaction between solvents and CDs was visualized. The results are shown in Fig. 9 . In the CYH solvent, there are only the van der Waals forces between all CDs and the solvent molecules. In contrast, in the DCM solvent, there are not only van der Waals forces, but also weak hydrogen bonds and halogen bonds, such as O…H-C hydrogen bond and O-H…Cl halogen bond for carboxyl-modified anthracene structure. Moreover, the intramolecular hydrogen bonds are also observed. For instance, the O atom in the carboxyl functional group creates an intramolecular hydrogen bond with the H in anthracene. In the MA solvent, the intramolecular hydrogen bonds exist and the MA molecules also forms an intermolecular hydrogen bond with the O atom in C=O. In fact, the intramolecular hydrogen bonds play a role in weakening the interaction between CDs and solvents. Therefore, both types of hydrogen bonds simultaneously act on the same O atom and thus there is a competition between them. Because intermolecular hydrogen bonds are stronger than the intramolecular ones, a red shift of the fluorescence emission is ultimately produced.

Fig. 10 shows the atomic heat maps of the anthracene structure modified with electron-withdrawing functional groups calculated by the microsolvent-SMD model. Fig. 10(a)-10(d) illustrate that for the three structures, as the solvent polarity increases, the distribution of electrons on the functional group gradually increases, the blue corresponding to the electron regions of C7 and C10 becomes darker, the blue corresponding to the hole regions of C11 and C14 becomes darker, but the color corresponding to the overlap regions of the four C atoms becomes lighter. This change reflects that the interaction between the CDs and the solvent lowers the π* energy level and promotes the π-π* transition of the CDs, resulting in a more pronounced separation of holes and electrons and thus a red shift in the fluorescence emission [62]. The change in color of these regions can also indicate that for the aldehyde- and ketone-modified anthracene structures with strong electron-withdrawing ability, the change in the charge distribution caused by hydrogen bonding is more pronounced, whereas for the carboxyl-modified anthracene structure with weak electron-withdrawing ability, a significant change is not observed in Fig. 10(e). From the non-polar CYH solvent to the high-polar MA solvent, the increase of electrons on the carboxyl group is only 1.2 %, and the increase of holes on the C7 and C8 on the anthracene is less than 1 % (Table S9). This suggests that the carboxyl group has a weak electron-withdrawing ability, and the influence of hydrogen bonding on the spectra is also minimal.

In order to verify the above conclusions, we prepared carbon dots modified with a large number of C=O groups by hydrothermal method and measured the fluorescence emission spectra of the samples under three solvents. The details about the experimental process are provided in the Supporting Information. The comparisons between experimental spectra and calculated results are shown in Fig. 11 .

Fig. 11 illustrates the comparison of experimental and calculated fluorescence spectra of ketone-modified anthracene structure in DCM, acetone (ACE) and MA solvents. Here, the solvent effect was calculated based on the microsolvent-SMD model. The order of polarity of the three solvents from lowest to highest is as follows: DCM, ACE, and MA. The results show that, the fluorescence spectra are gradually red shifted from the DCM solvent to the MA solvent with the enhancement of the solvent polarity and the interactions between the CD and the solvent. The experimentally measured and computationally calculated spectra show a very close degree of redshift. This result supports the conclusion that the microsolvent-SMD model can emphasize the interaction between the solvent molecules and surface functional groups of CDs, and confirms that the stronger the interaction, the more pronounced the modulation of the spectra. It can be seen that it is more advantageous to use the improved solvent model to study the spectral modulation of CDs by solvent effects, which can provide better theoretical support for the experimental design and synthesis of high-performance CDs using the solvent solvatochromism mechanism.

In this work, the fluorescence properties of CDs in three solvents (CYH, DCM, MA) have been investigated by using DFT and TD-DFT method. In order to fully understand the solvent effect, the implicit SMD model and the microsolvent-SMD model are employed for calculations.

Calculations using the SMD model demonstrate that the solvent polarity plays a crucial role in shifting the fluorescence spectra of the CDs. As the solvent polarity increases, the HOMO-LUMO gap of the CDs decreases, and the fluorescence emission experiences a red shift. However, the calculations using the microsolvent-SMD model indicate that the solvent polarity and the interaction between the CDs and the solvent molecules synergistically affect the fluorescence properties. The stronger the electron-donating or electron-withdrawing ability of the functional groups in the CDs, the more pronounced the solvent effect and the fluorescence emission shift.

In this work, the microsolvent-SMD model emphasizes the interactions between solvent molecules and functional groups, including van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonds or halogen bonds. The impact of hydrogen bonds on the fluorescence properties of CDs can be divided into two cases. If the fluorescence emission of CDs mainly originates from π-π* transition, then hydrogen bonds can promote π-π* transition, the degree of charge transfer of CDs would be enhanced, the fluorescence emission shift is more evident. In this case, the effect of interaction between CDs and solvent is greater than the impact of the solvent polarity. In the second case, the fluorescence emission of CDs is mainly derived from the n-π* transition. When an atom containing a lone pair of electrons within a functional group of CD forms a hydrogen bond with a polar solvent, the contribution of this atom to the electronic transition would be lessened. Correspondingly, the energy required for the n-π* transition of the CDs increases, and the degree of charge transfer of the CDs decreases, and finally, a blue shift occurs in the fluorescence spectrum.

The microsolvent-SMD model can comprehensively analyze the solvent polarity and the interaction between solvent molecules and CDs, revealing the process of solvent molecules participating in fluorescence emission, and the calculated results are more consistent with the experimental values. Therefore, this work enriches the understanding of the solvent effect, establishes a theoretical foundation for using solvents to regulate the fluorescence emission of CDs, and provides an effective pathway for facilitating controlled synthesis of CDs.

Xiaojie Ren: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Li Guan: Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Weicai Shi: Formal analysis, Software. Jianen Zhang: Formal analysis, Methodology, Software. Mingjun Chen: Data curation, Validation. Tao Luo: Formal analysis. Chunzhi Liu: Investigation. Youshi Lan: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zhijuan Chen: Formal analysis. Xiaobo Chen: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xu Li: Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

We also appreciate High Performance Supercomputer Center of Hebei University.
